focus: Platform switching triggers and competitive dynamics

findings.hypothesis_tested: Multi-platform usage among students is primarily price/promotion-driven rather than occasion-based, with subscription bundling as the key lock-in mechanism
findings.verdict: CONFIRMED - Price/promotion is the primary trigger, but subscription ecosystem creates ecosystem-driven retention

price_driven_switching.description: Students switch between platforms based on which has the best current deal, promotion, or lowest total cost

segments.text: I mean, if it's only €2 of a difference, it would be whichever app I come across first... so I probably wouldn't be searching for, like, an alternative. If it's only €2... what about €5? I probably would be more inclined to switch. Yeah!

If I was in my flat, there's like a little Sainsbury's local, honestly, like less than a minute away, so I just be very likely just to go across because that's going to be quicker and cheaper than paying Uber to deliver it for me.

So for another company for me to switch, they would have to be a lot more than just being like a food delivery app. Like I use Uber Eats more than anything else, but I use it because I was already using Uber for everything else.

So in most cases if I'd like gone on to Uber and it had the restaurant, I wouldn't have checked anywhere else, so I wouldn't have known. But in this like scenario, I'd go for the cheaper one. If everything was the same and it was like the same delivery time frame, I'd go for the cheaper one.

Yeah, I say takeaways, like, once. Once every couple of weeks. Maybe once a week if I've had, like, a particularly, like, grueling week. But grocery deliveries, I'd say. I know. I order the. I ordered, like. Yeah. Groceries on delivery or Ubereats every now and then. Not very often, I'd guess maybe

subscription_lock_in.description: Subscription holders default to their subscribed platform to maximize value, even when competitors have better individual deals

restaurant_availability.description: Some platform switching is driven by restaurant exclusivity or availability differences

findings.new_insight: Platform switching is primarily price/promotion-driven rather than occasion-based. 28/33 participants use multiple platforms. Subscription lock-in is the strongest single factor preventing switching — students default to their subscribed platform even when alternatives offer better individual deals. Deliveroo's competitive advantage is perceived restaurant quality and variety, not exclusive loyalty.
findings.recommendation_update: 1) Accept multi-homing as reality and focus on owning high-value occasions. 2) Strengthen subscription lock-in through better Plus benefits. 3) Leverage restaurant quality/variety perception as key differentiator. 4) Price-match or beat competitor promotions during acquisition windows (freshers, exam periods).
